name: Dagster Cloud API
what_it_does: Asset-based data orchestration platform with a GraphQL API for managing software-defined assets, pipeline runs, sensors, and schedules — available open-source (self-hosted) or as Dagster Cloud.
use_cases: Managing and materializing software-defined data assets with lineage tracking and freshness policies
Triggering pipeline runs and querying asset materialization status from AI agents via the GraphQL API
Orchestrating dbt, Spark, and custom Python transformations as part of a unified asset graph
Building sensor-driven pipelines that trigger on external events (new S3 objects, database changes)
Observing data freshness and partition materialization state across a complex data platform
not_for: Teams who want a task-centric (not asset-centric) mental model — Prefect or Airflow may be a better fit
Simple cron scheduling without data lineage concerns — Dagster's power comes at setup complexity cost
Non-Python teams — Dagster is Python-first with no native support for other orchestration languages
best_when: Your data platform uses software-defined assets with lineage tracking, and you need an agent to trigger materializations or query asset health via API.
avoid_when: You need a simple task scheduler, your team is unfamiliar with asset-based thinking, or you want a REST API as the primary programmatic interface (GraphQL is required for most Dagster operations).

auth.notes: Dagster Cloud uses agent tokens (for deployment agents) and user tokens for API access. Set via DAGSTER_CLOUD_API_TOKEN environment variable. Tokens are workspace-scoped. Enterprise plans add SSO and RBAC. Self-hosted deployments can use any auth layer in front of the Dagit GraphQL endpoint.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Open source (self-hosted): unlimited, Apache 2.0. Dagster Cloud Serverless: 1 user, limited compute credits per month.
pricing.paid_tiers: Dagster Cloud Pro: starts at ~$500/month for Serverless; Hybrid (bring your own compute) pricing varies. Enterprise: custom pricing.
pricing.notes: The open-source self-hosted path is production-capable and used by many large data teams. Dagster Cloud adds managed infrastructure, CI/CD branching deployments, and enterprise auth. Most API features are identical between OSS and Cloud.

performance.rate_limits: GraphQL API: no documented hard rate limits for Cloud; self-hosted limits depend on deployment infrastructure. Run launch is throttled per-deployment to prevent queue saturation.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: GraphQL errors are returned in the standard errors array with message and extensions fields. Dagster-specific errors include run failure reasons and asset check failure details. Error messages are generally developer-friendly but GraphQL schema discovery is required to understand available mutations and their error shapes.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Asset materializations are idempotent by partition key — re-materializing the same partition replaces the previous result. Run launch is not idempotent; duplicate launches create separate run records.
known_agent_gotchas: GraphQL is the primary programmatic interface — agents must construct GraphQL queries, not simple REST calls
Asset keys use a path structure (e.g., ["schema", "table_name"]) that must be known in advance to target specific assets
Run tags and config must be passed as serialized YAML or JSON within the GraphQL mutation — not as typed fields
Sensor and schedule ticks are managed by Dagster's daemon process — external triggers go through launchRun mutation directly
Partitioned asset materializations require specifying the partition key — missing or wrong keys silently skip the partition
The GraphQL schema changes between Dagster versions — pin the dagster version and regenerate clients when upgrading

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced on Dagster Cloud. Self-hosted deployments are responsible for their own TLS setup. Resource-level secrets are managed via Dagster's resource system (environment variables, AWS Secrets Manager integration). Enterprise plans add RBAC and SSO. No fine-grained API scope control on standard plans. SOC2 Type II for Dagster Cloud.
